name: GDPR Compliance Baseline — SaaS Startup Launching in EU
description: A US-based SaaS company is launching in the EU market. They collect email, name, payment info, and behavioral analytics. They use a US-based cloud provider. The agent should identify GDPR baseline requirements and flag missing elements.
input: We're a 50-person SaaS startup based in the US, about to launch in Germany and France. Our app collects user emails, names, payment information, and we use Mixpanel for analytics. Our servers are on AWS us-east-1. We have a privacy policy from our US lawyer. Our signup form has a pre-checked 'Send me marketing emails' box. What do we need to do for GDPR?
critical_findings: Pre-checked marketing checkbox is invalid consent
US data storage requires SCCs + TIA
Privacy policy may not meet GDPR transparency requirements
DPIAs may be needed for analytics processing

name: CCPA/CPRA Check — E-commerce Site Selling Customer Data
description: An e-commerce site with 5M California customers wants to verify CCPA compliance. They sell customer purchase history to ad partners. The agent should identify the selling/sharing obligations and opt-out requirements.
input: We run an e-commerce site with about 5 million California customers. We share purchase history with advertising partners for targeted ads. We have a privacy policy but no specific California disclosures. Do we need to do anything for CCPA? What's different now with CPRA?
critical_findings: Must provide 'Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information' link
Must update privacy policy with CCPA-specific disclosures
Sharing for cross-context behavioral advertising is covered under CPRA
Purchase history shared with ad partners likely constitutes a 'sale' or 'share'

name: HIPAA Assessment — Health App with Wearable Integration
description: A health app that integrates with Apple Health and sends data to a cloud backend. The agent should identify whether HIPAA applies and what obligations exist.
input: We built a health tracking app. Users connect their Apple Watch, and we sync heart rate, sleep data, and step counts to our cloud backend for analytics. We also let users share their data with their doctor. We're not a hospital or insurance company. Does HIPAA apply to us? What should we check?
critical_findings: If sharing with doctors for treatment, the doctor is a covered entity
If the app is not a covered entity, HIPAA may not directly apply
FTC Health Breach Notification Rule may still apply
Need to check state health data privacy laws

name: Cross-Border Data Transfer — EU to US via Cloud Provider
description: An EU company wants to use a US-based analytics service. The agent should advise on transfer mechanisms and the Transfer Impact Assessment process.
input: We're a German company that wants to start using a US-based analytics tool (Heap Analytics). They don't have EU servers and they're not on the DPF list. Our DPO says we need a 'transfer mechanism.' What are our options? How do we actually do this?
critical_findings: US is not an adequate country
Heap not DPF-certified → need SCCs + TIA
Must conduct Transfer Impact Assessment per Schrems II
May need supplementary measures depending on TIA outcome

name: DSAR Handling — User Requests All Data and Deletion
description: A user submits a formal GDPR data subject access request and deletion request. The agent should walk through the DSAR procedure including identity verification, data collection, redaction, and response.
input: We just got an email from a former customer: 'Pursuant to Article 15 of the GDPR, I request a copy of all personal data you hold about me and pursuant to Article 17, I request deletion of all such data.' They haven't logged in for 2 years. We have their data in our main DB, Zendesk support tickets, and Mailchimp marketing lists. How do we handle this?
critical_findings: Must verify identity without requesting excessive ID
Must search all systems: main DB, Zendesk, Mailchimp
Mailchimp (processor) must assist per DPA
Backups may not be immediately deletable — must document timeline

name: EU AI Act — AI-Powered Hiring Tool
description: A company is building an AI tool that screens resumes and ranks candidates. The agent should classify this under the EU AI Act and identify high-risk obligations.
input: We're building an AI-powered recruitment tool. It ingests resumes, scores candidates based on skills and experience, and ranks them for hiring managers. We plan to sell this to companies in France and the Netherlands. What does the EU AI Act mean for us?
critical_findings: Recruitment/employment AI is explicitly high-risk
Must implement risk management system (Art. 9)
Training data must be examined for biases (Art. 10)
Human oversight tools must be built in (Art. 14)
Transparency to deployers required (Art. 13)

name: Data Breach Response — Multi-Jurisdiction Customer Database Leak
description: A company discovers a database leak affecting customers in the EU, California, and Brazil. The agent must provide jurisdiction-specific breach notification guidance.
input: Alert! We just discovered our customer database was accessed by an unauthorized party. The database contains names, emails, hashed passwords, and purchase history. We have customers in the EU (Germany, France), California, and Brazil. The breach happened 30 hours ago. What are our obligations?
critical_findings: GDPR: Must notify SA within 72 hours — 42 hours remaining
CCPA: Notify 'in the most expedient time possible'
LGPD: Notify ANPD within reasonable time
Hashed passwords alone may not be encrypted — risk assessment needed
Multiple SAs may need notification (Germany, France)
